attribution.name: Bonifacio Bembo (workshop)
attribution.note: Painter of the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) Tarot, Milan

attribution.name: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale
attribution.note: Holds the 67 surviving Cary-Yale cards (Cary Collection of Playing Cards)

name: Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot — The Lavish Outlier (c. 1442)
description:
The most lavish and most unusual of the surviving hand-painted tarots. Around 1442 the workshop of Bonifacio Bembo painted the Cary-Yale, or Visconti di Modrone, deck in Milan — probably for the marriage of Bianca Maria Visconti and Francesco Sforza. It is built here only from the cards that survive (67, held at Yale's Beinecke Library), because the deck is famous precisely for what it contains.

Two features set it apart from every other tarot. First, its courts have SIX ranks, not four — King, Queen, Knight, Knave, and, uniquely, a female Horsewoman and a female Damsel — so its court is half women. The 'Damsel of Swords' is one of these. Second, it adds the three THEOLOGICAL virtues — Faith, Hope, and Charity — to the usual cardinal virtues, found in no other tarot, turning the deck into a complete medieval moral cosmos. In its original state it may have held 86 cards.

Each card carries an Iconography note (what Bembo painted), a History note, and a light Reading. This is a historical/iconographic grammar, not a divination deck. It is the sister of the Pierpont Morgan-Bergamo (Visconti-Sforza) and Brera-Brambilla decks from the same workshop.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Bonifacio Bembo workshop, Cary-Yale / Visconti di Modrone Tarot, c. 1442 (Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale, Cary Collection) — gold-ground tempera, International Gothic. All on Wikimedia Commons, Category:Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: An enthroned, bearded emperor in gold brocade holding an orb, the imperial eagle on display, attendants at his feet.
sections.History: One of the few trumps to survive in this deck. The Cary-Yale was likely made for the 1441 marriage of Bianca Maria Visconti and Francesco Sforza; its emperor is dynastic propaganda in gold.
sections.Reading: Temporal authority and the architecture of rule — the masculine throne the whole procession serves.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A crowned empress enthroned in brocade, bearing a shield and sceptre, ladies in attendance.
sections.History: A surviving trump. The Cary-Yale's courtly women are rendered with unusual individuality — part of the deck's preoccupation with the feminine that also produced its female knights and pages.
sections.Reading: Sovereign abundance, the generative authority that rules from the throne.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A couple beneath a draped pavilion clasp hands while a blindfolded Cupid hovers above on a column; a dog and attendants frame the scene.
sections.History: Among the most celebrated surviving Cary-Yale cards — very probably a portrait of the Visconti-Sforza wedding the deck was made to honour.
sections.Reading: Union as alliance, love bound to lineage — desire formalised into a marriage of houses.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A crowned noblewoman rides a canopied triumphal car drawn by two white horses, attendants walking alongside.
sections.History: The card that names the game (trionfo / triumph). The Cary-Yale chariot is driven by a woman, in keeping with the deck's foregrounding of female figures.
sections.Reading: Triumph and momentum — the self borne forward in a victory parade over what it has mastered.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A figure in courtly dress overpowers a lion, wrenching open or subduing its jaws — the Herculean image of fortitude.
sections.History: A surviving cardinal virtue. In the Cary-Yale the cardinal virtues sit alongside the rare theological virtues (Faith, Hope, Charity) — an unusually complete moral programme.
sections.Reading: Fortitude — strength of soul that masters the beast through steady will rather than rage.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A mounted or standing skeletal figure with a great bow, riding across the gold over fallen bodies.
sections.History: A surviving trump, here given the dignity of full gold-ground treatment — Death as a noble rider, not a grotesque.
sections.Reading: The great leveller — the end that rides through every estate without exception.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: Trumpeting angels above; below, the dead rise from their tombs at the summons, hands lifted.
sections.History: A surviving trump. The Last Judgement is the most overtly Christian of the triumphs and a natural companion to the deck's theological virtues.
sections.Reading: The call and the reckoning — awakening, summons, the dead rising to answer.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A crowned figure (Fame, or a lady) presides above a landscape with a walled city, knights, and ship — the world spread out to be surveyed and won.
sections.History: The highest triumph. The Cary-Yale's World is more a panorama of earthly glory than the later cosmic wreath — fitting for a dynastic deck.
sections.Reading: Completion and dominion — the whole world held up to be seen and possessed.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A woman holds a chalice and cross, sometimes with a kneeling figure — the personification of Faith (Fides).
sections.History: One of the three THEOLOGICAL virtues found in no other tarot. Their presence suggests the Cary-Yale was conceived as a complete moral cosmos — the four cardinal plus the three theological virtues.
sections.Reading: Faith — trust that orients the soul beyond what it can prove, the first of the theological virtues.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A woman gazes upward with hands clasped in prayer, often with an anchor — the personification of Hope (Spes).
sections.History: A theological virtue unique to this deck. The anchor is Hope's ancient attribute; here it joins the tarot for the only time in its history.
sections.Reading: Hope — the upward reach that endures through the dark, the anchor of the soul.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

metadata.museum: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University

sections.Iconography: A woman nurses or tends children, sometimes with a flame — the personification of Charity (Caritas), greatest of the three.
sections.History: The third and greatest theological virtue, unique to the Cary-Yale. Charity nursing children is the classic Caritas image carried over from devotional painting.
sections.Reading: Charity — love that gives itself away, named by tradition the greatest of the virtues.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Ace of Coins: 1 gold-and-colour coin emblem arranged with scrolling decoration on a tooled gold ground — a decorative pip, not a narrative scene.
sections.History: A surviving pip of Coins (Denari), the suit of Earth, painted for the game of trionfi.
sections.Reading: Ace of Coins — the pure root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within the material world — money, body, and provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.History: A surviving pip of Coins (Denari), the suit of Earth, painted for the game of trionfi.
sections.Reading: Two of Coins — duality and exchange — pairing, the first relationship, within the material world — money, body, and provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Damsel of Coins: a courtly figure of the Visconti-Sforza house in gold brocade bearing the emblem of Coins. The Cary-Yale is unique for its six-rank courts — King, Queen, Knight, Horsewoman, Knave, and Damsel — half of them women.
sections.History: A surviving court card of Coins (Denari). The female Horsewoman and Damsel ranks that swell these courts appear in almost no other tarot, and gave the deck 86 cards in its original state.
sections.Reading: Damsel of Coins — the female page — a court rank found almost only in this deck, the maiden of the suit, within the material world — money, body, and provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.History: A surviving court card of Coins (Denari). The female Horsewoman and Damsel ranks that swell these courts appear in almost no other tarot, and gave the deck 86 cards in its original state.
sections.Reading: Knight of Coins — the male rider — the suit questing, active, in motion, within the material world — money, body, and provision.

sections.Reading: Horsewoman of Coins — the female rider — a female knight unique to the Cary-Yale, the suit's energy embodied in a noblewoman on horseback, within the material world — money, body, and provision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Reading: Queen of Coins — the inward sovereignty of the suit — it held, nurtured, understood, within the material world — money, body, and provision.

sections.Reading: King of Coins — the outward authority of the suit — it commanded and ruled in the world, within the material world — money, body, and provision.

sections.Reading: Two of Swords — duality and exchange — pairing, the first relationship, within mind, conflict, and decision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Iconography: Damsel of Swords: a courtly figure of the Visconti-Sforza house in gold brocade bearing the emblem of Swords. The Cary-Yale is unique for its six-rank courts — King, Queen, Knight, Horsewoman, Knave, and Damsel — half of them women.
sections.History: A surviving court card of Swords (Spade). The female Horsewoman and Damsel ranks that swell these courts appear in almost no other tarot, and gave the deck 86 cards in its original state.
sections.Reading: Damsel of Swords — the female page — a court rank found almost only in this deck, the maiden of the suit, within mind, conflict, and decision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.History: A surviving court card of Swords (Spade). The female Horsewoman and Damsel ranks that swell these courts appear in almost no other tarot, and gave the deck 86 cards in its original state.
sections.Reading: Horsewoman of Swords — the female rider — a female knight unique to the Cary-Yale, the suit's energy embodied in a noblewoman on horseback, within mind, conflict, and decision.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Reading: Ace of Batons — the pure root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within will, work, and growth.

sections.About these Cards: Only a handful of the Cary-Yale's triumphs survive: the Emperor, Empress, Lovers, Chariot, Strength, Death, Judgement, and the World. The rest are lost. What remains is enough to show a deck of extraordinary richness — full gold grounds, individualised faces, dynastic pride.
sections.How to Use: Read these as the fragments of a once-complete procession of estates, made to celebrate a ducal marriage.

items.name: The Theological Virtues — Faith, Hope, Charity

sections.About these Cards: The Cary-Yale alone among tarots adds the three THEOLOGICAL virtues — Faith, Hope, and Charity — to the usual cardinal virtues. No other tarot deck contains them. They turn the deck into a complete medieval moral cosmos.
sections.Why this Matters: These three cards are the single strongest sign that the early tarot trumps were conceived as a moral and cosmological ladder, not a random gallery — and a reminder of how much the 'standard' 22-trump sequence is a later simplification.

items.name: Suit of Coins — with the Six-Rank Court

sections.About this Suit: The surviving Coins cards of the Cary-Yale. Like all four suits here, its court runs to six ranks — King, Queen, Knight, Horsewoman, Knave, Damsel — doubling the usual nobility with female riders and pages.
sections.How to Use: Browse the suit to see the Cary-Yale's defining peculiarity: a court half made of women, unmatched in any other historical tarot.
